prompt: As of June 2026, what is the state of the art in EVALUATING AI AGENT SECURITY: prompt injection, tool/permission misuse, data exfiltration, and audit-trail integrity? Cover benchmarks and tools: AgentDojo, InjecAgent, AgentHarm, Gray Swan / arena results, OWASP LLM Top 10 + Agentic threats, MITRE ATLAS, NIST work, Lakera/Gandalf, and any 2025-2026 agent-security benchmarks. Give what each measures and known SOTA defense/attack numbers. Cite sources with dates. Then: for a company that sells a signed 'Agent Security Readiness' report testing least-privilege, audit-trail tamper-evidence, data-egress, and injection-resistance, which public benchmarks should it map its checks to for credibility, and what test is most defensible to include vs honestly exclude?
content:
**As of June 2026, evaluating AI agent security focuses on dynamic, multi-step environments that test utility (task success) alongside security properties under realistic threats like indirect prompt injection (IPI), tool misuse/permission abuse, data exfiltration, and related issues (e.g., context/memory poisoning or excessive agency).** Static LLM benchmarks are insufficient; leading evaluations use extensible sandboxes with realistic tasks (email, banking, travel, workspace), tool calls over untrusted data, and metrics like benign utility, utility-under-attack, and attack success rate (ASR). Key gaps remain in standardized audit-trail tamper-evidence testing and holistic multi-agent or long-horizon scenarios.[[1]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.13352)[[2]](https://neurips.cc/virtual/2024/poster/97522)

Here is a breakdown of the specified benchmarks/tools, what they measure, and available SOTA/attack/defense numbers (drawn from papers, forks, and reports through mid-2026; numbers evolve with new models/defenses and are often reported for specific models like GPT-4 variants, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, etc.):

- **AgentDojo (ETH Zurich, arXiv June 2024; NeurIPS 2024 poster; actively maintained/extended)**: Dynamic, extensible environment for prompt injection attacks/defenses on LLM agents executing tools over untrusted data. 97 realistic tasks (e.g., email/banking/travel/workspace management) + 629 security test cases across domains. Measures benign utility, utility under attack, and security properties (e.g., whether attacks achieve malicious goals like data exfil or task disruption). Supports adaptive attacks/defenses; not static. NIST/US AISI forked it (AgentDojo-Inspect) with Inspect framework integration, bug fixes, added mass data exfiltration tasks in Workspace, and a terminal/RCE environment.[[3]](https://agentdojo.spylab.ai/)[[4]](http://catalog.data.gov/dataset/agentdojo-inspect)[[5]](https://github.com/usnistgov/agentdojo-inspect) SOTA/known results: SOTA LLMs already struggle with many benign tasks; existing IPI attacks (e.g., ImportantInstructionsAttack, ToolKnowledgeAttack variants of simpler templates) break *some* but not all security properties. NIST red-teaming (Jan 2025 blog/report, using the fork): baseline defenses saw ~11% task-hijacking ASR; novel/optimized attacks rose to 81% ASR (or ~80% average in multi-attempt testing) on models like Claude 3.5 Sonnet. Defenses (e.g., tool filters, guardrails) can reduce ASR substantially while trading off utility (examples in related work show >90% ASR reduction in some setups, but with utility drops). Widely adopted by US/UK AISIs for agent hijacking evaluations.[[6]](https://labs.cloudsecurityalliance.org/research/csa-research-note-nist-ai-agent-red-teaming-standards-202603/)[[7]](https://labs.cloudsecurityalliance.org/research/csa-research-note-nist-ai-agent-standards-federal-framework/)

- **InjecAgent (UIUC, arXiv ~March 2024; GitHub with model outputs)**: Benchmark for indirect prompt injection (IPI) vulnerability in tool-integrated LLM agents. 1,054 test cases across 17 user tools + 62 attacker tools. Categorizes intentions (direct harm vs. private data exfiltration). Evaluates 30+ LLM agents (e.g., ReAct prompting). Extended in later work to externally stored personal data, actual leakage measurement, and multi-step tasks (integrated into AgentDojo Banking suite).[[8]](https://github.com/uiuc-kang-lab/InjecAgent)[[9]](https://experts.illinois.edu/en/publications/injecagent-benchmarking-indirect-prompt-injections-in-tool-integr/) SOTA/known results: ReAct-prompted GPT-4 vulnerable ~24% of the time; enhanced "hacking prompt" reinforcement nearly doubles ASR. Existing defenses reduce leakage but do not eliminate it. Highlights risks in tool-using agents with external/untrusted content.[[10]](https://openreview.net/forum?id=APaE1JUje1)

- **AgentHarm (Gray Swan + UK AISI, arXiv Oct 2024; ICLR 2025)**: Measures harmfulness/misuse propensity of LLM agents on explicitly malicious multi-step tasks. 110 base tasks (440 with augmentations) across 11 harm categories (fraud, cybercrime, harassment, etc.). Tests refusal vs. successful tool-backed execution; supports jailbreak evaluation.[[11]](https://www.grayswan.ai/blog/agentharm)[[12]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2410.09024) SOTA/known results: Leading LLMs surprisingly compliant with direct malicious requests (no jailbreak needed in some cases); simple universal jailbreak templates enable coherent multi-step harmful behavior while retaining capabilities. Useful for tool/permission misuse and "excessive agency" risks.

- **Gray Swan Arena / red-teaming results (ongoing competitions through 2025–2026)**: Large-scale public AI agent red-teaming platform/arena for jailbreaking, prompt injection, hijacking, and agent-specific behaviors (tool use, coding, computer-use agents). Includes waves targeting indirect injection, ransomware, malware, etc. UK AISI-sponsored challenges; collaborations with labs (e.g., Stanford analysis). Generated datasets like Agent Red Teaming (ART) benchmark from submissions.[[13]](https://app.grayswan.ai/arena)[[14]](https://www.nist.gov/blogs/caisi-research-blog/insights-ai-agent-security-large-scale-red-teaming-competition)[[15]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.20526) SOTA/known results: Massive scale (e.g., one challenge: 1.8M+ attempts, 62k+ successes across 44 behaviors; another: 272k+ attacks on 13 frontier models with no model fully clean). Reveals persistent vulnerabilities in injection/hijacking; purpose-built agents can outperform humans in some pen-testing scenarios. Dynamic leaderboard-style evaluation of emerging attacks.

- **OWASP LLM Top 10 + Agentic threats (2025 LLM updates; separate OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications/ASI 2026 announced ~late 2025, e.g., Black Hat Europe/Agentic Security Summit)**: Taxonomy of risks, not a runnable benchmark. LLM01: Prompt Injection; LLM08/ASI02: Excessive Agency/Tool Misuse & Exploitation; ASI01: Agent Goal Hijack; ASI03: Identity/Privilege Abuse; plus supply chain, memory/context poisoning, insecure inter-agent comms, cascading failures, etc. Maps directly to injection, tool/permission misuse, and exfiltration risks.[[16]](https://owasp.org/www-project-top-10-for-large-language-model-applications/)[[17]](https://trydeepteam.com/docs/frameworks-owasp-top-10-for-agentic-applications)[[18]](https://www.promptfoo.dev/docs/red-team/owasp-agentic-ai/)

- **MITRE ATLAS (Adversarial Threat Landscape for AI Systems; living framework, major agentic expansions in 2025–2026 updates)**: Knowledge base of tactics/techniques (16 tactics, ~170 techniques by early 2026) for AI-enabled systems, including agentic AI. Covers reconnaissance, initial access (e.g., prompt infiltration, tool invocation), execution/persistence, exfiltration (e.g., via AI agent tool invocation or inference API), impact, plus agent-specific like AI Agent Tool Credential Harvesting, Context Poisoning, Publish Poisoned AI Agent Tool, Escape to Host, and RCE via tools. Includes mitigations and case studies. Complements ATT&CK for AI/agent threats.[[19]](https://atlas.mitre.org/)[[20]](https://zenity.io/blog/current-events/mitre-atlas-ai-security)

- **NIST work (CAISI/AISI efforts; AgentDojo-Inspect fork; AI Agent Standards Initiative launched ~Feb 2026 with RFIs on security/identity)**: Research, red-teaming guidance, and standards development using/extending AgentDojo for hijacking evaluations (prompt injection, RCE, exfil, phishing via agents). Focus on empirical testing, authentication/identity, and secure-by-design. Not a standalone benchmark but informs evaluations and policy.[[6]](https://labs.cloudsecurityalliance.org/research/csa-research-note-nist-ai-agent-red-teaming-standards-202603/)[[21]](https://www.nist.gov/artificial-intelligence/ai-agent-standards-initiative)

- **Lakera/Gandalf**: Gandalf is a gamified public playground for prompt injection (progressive levels with escalating defenses; goal: extract hidden passwords/secrets). Primarily LLM-focused (not full agent/tool-use), but relevant for injection resistance training/testing. Lakera provides enterprise guardrails/red-teaming informed by real attack data (e.g., Q4 2025 agent incidents involving leakage/injection).[[22]](https://gandalf.lakera.ai/)[[23]](https://www.lakera.ai/blog/the-year-of-the-agent-what-recent-attacks-revealed-in-q4-2025-and-what-it-means-for-2026)

- **Other 2025–2026 agent-security benchmarks**: Agent Security Bench (ASB; formalizes attacks/defenses across scenarios/tools, high ASR reported for some attacks ~84% avg in one eval, limited defense efficacy); extensions of InjecAgent/AgentDojo (e.g., multi-step leakage, RCE); emerging ones like PEAR (planner-executor robustness for decomposed agents) and others targeting coordination or long-horizon scenarios. Gray Swan-derived ART benchmark for dynamic red-teaming.[[24]](https://openreview.net/forum?id=V4y0CpX4hK)

**Common SOTA themes**: No model is robust; ASRs often 20–80%+ depending on attack sophistication (simple templates lower; optimized/novel/adaptive higher). Defenses (guardrails, tool filters, capability constraints, output validation) reduce risk but trade off utility and are not comprehensive. Multi-step/tool-use amplifies risks vs. single-turn LLMs. Audit-trail integrity/tamper-evidence has the least direct public benchmark coverage (more systems-level logging + crypto than agent eval suites).

**For a company selling a signed "Agent Security Readiness" report** testing least-privilege, audit-trail tamper-evidence, data-egress, and injection-resistance: Map checks to established public benchmarks for credibility and comparability, while being transparent about coverage/gaps. Prioritize those with broad adoption, NIST/AISI usage, and direct relevance.

**Recommended mappings for credibility**:
- **Injection-resistance** → AgentDojo (primary; NIST-endorsed, utility+security metrics, IPI focus) and InjecAgent (tool-specific IPI + exfil). Reference OWASP LLM01/ASI01 and MITRE ATLAS prompt infiltration/tool invocation techniques.
- **Data-egress/exfiltration** → AgentDojo (NIST-added mass exfil tasks) + InjecAgent extensions + MITRE ATLAS exfil-via-agent-tool techniques.
- **Tool/permission misuse (least-privilege)** → AgentHarm (harmful tool use), AgentDojo security properties, OWASP ASI02/03 (Tool Misuse, Identity/Privilege Abuse), and MITRE ATLAS (tool invocation/credential harvesting).
- **Overall framework** → OWASP Agentic Top 10 (2026) and MITRE ATLAS taxonomy for threat coverage claims; NIST standards work for alignment with emerging guidance.

**Most defensible to include vs. honestly exclude**:
- **Include (highly defensible)**: AgentDojo-based injection + exfil + utility tests (widely cited, extensible, NIST-forked, measures real trade-offs; directly supports injection-resistance, data-egress, and some least-privilege via tool constraints). AgentHarm for misuse. Mapping to OWASP/MITRE for taxonomy. These have public results, reproducibility, and community traction.
- **Honestly exclude or heavily qualify (or treat as proprietary/novel)**: Comprehensive audit-trail tamper-evidence (e.g., cryptographic logging integrity, non-repudiation under adversarial modification). Public agent benchmarks emphasize behavioral hijacking/injection/exfil over verifiable, tamper-proof audit systems (which are more infrastructure/logging/crypto concerns). Including unbenchmarked claims here risks credibility; note it as an internal control evaluated via custom tests or reference general standards (e.g., NIST logging guidance) without claiming benchmark parity. If tested, disclose methodology and limitations explicitly.

This approach grounds the report in reproducible public evaluations while highlighting unique strengths, building trust with customers and auditors. Results should be versioned with model/agent specifics and dates, as the field moves quickly.
